CSCE 331 Syllabus Section 914 (57938) Foundations of Software Engineering Fall 2025 - College Station Course Information This syllabus covers sections 912-915. Meeting Times:  Meeting Type: LEC Meeting Days: MWF Start Time: 09:10AM End Time: 10:00AM Start Date: 08/25/2025 End Date: 12/16/2025 Meeting Location:  ZACH 310 Meeting Times: (Section 912)  Meeting Type: LAB Meeting Days: MW Start Time: 5:45PM End Time: 6:35PM Start Date: 08/25/2025 End Date: 12/16/2025 Meeting Location:  ZACH 596 Meeting Times: (Section 913)  Meeting Type: LAB Meeting Days: MW Start Time: 4:10PM End Time: 5:00PM Meeting Location:  ZACH 596 Meeting Times: (Section 914)  Meeting Type: LAB Meeting Days: MW Start Time: 5:45PM End Time: 6:35PM Meeting Location:  ZACH 596 Meeting Times: (Section 915)  Meeting Type: LAB
Meeting Days: MW
Start Time: 7:00PM
End Time: 7:50PM Meeting Location:  ZACH 596 Finals : You are required to come to both the final for your lecture and the final for your lab. All final times are set by the registrar and can be found here. Credit Hours:  4 Instructor Details Robert Lightfoot Email: rob.light@tamu.edu Office: 422 Peterson Phone: 9798452611 Office Hours Posted in Canvas Preferred Contact Method Use Canvas inbox for all course-related correspondence. Webpage: https://engineering.tamu.edu/cse/profiles/Lightfoot- Robert.html Biography Robert Lightfoot earned his Ph.D. in Multidisciplinary Engineering and B.S. in Computer Science from Texas A&M University, and an M.S. in Computer Science from Southern Methodist University. His career includes work at Ericsson and DSC (Motorola) and independent development of smart sorting systems. His teaching and research focus on software engineering and computer science education. Socials Study abroad and Fish camp are amazing! Course Description Foundations in Software Engineering: CSCE 331 Intensive programming experience and provision of the fundamentals needed for larger-scale software development; integration of concepts in computer science and familiarization with a variety of programming and development tools and techniques; team projects each with an emphasis on a different specialization within computer science; emphasis on programming techniques to ease code integration and clarity; practical exposure to software-engineering processes through large-scale projects and specification and documentation.

in computer science and familiarization with a variety of programming

and development tools and techniques; team projects each with an

emphasis on a different specialization within computer science;

emphasis on programming techniques to ease code integration and

clarity; practical exposure to software-engineering processes through

large-scale projects and specification and documentation.

Course Prerequisites

Prerequisite/Corequisite(s):  Grade of C or better in CSCE 314,

CSCE 350/ECEN 350, or ECEN 350/CSCE 350; grade of C or better or concurrent

enrollment in CSCE 313.

-

If you have not yet mastered the concepts in these courses, you

should visit me or a TA. We can provide resources to help you learn

and review them. Mastery of prerequisite concepts is important as this

course will build on that knowledge and grow your understanding of

programming and computer systems.

For students taking CSCE 313 concurrently:  If you decide to q-drop

CSCE 313 to defer it to a future semester, you will not be able to

remain in this course. Both courses are needed to satisfy the

curriculum's overall design and to prepare you for later CSCE technical

courses.

Special Course Designation

UWRT - University Required Writing Intensive

Designation : W (writing-intensive course)

W (writing-intensive course):

All Texas A&M University undergraduates must pass two courses in

their major that fulfill the graduation requirement for writing and oral

communication. These W/C courses support you in becoming a

proficient writer and speaker in our discipline.

This course is a writing-intensive (W) course. You will practice the types

of writing you will encounter in the workplace or academic careers in

our discipline.  As a writing-intensive course, you must pass the

writing components with a C or better to pass the course.

This course provides writing instruction and multiple opportunities to

develop and practice your skills. You will also receive formative

feedback from which you can revise your writing.

It is normal for writing to be challenging, especially when learning to

tailor it for a particular discipline. Writing, just like software

development, is a process that involves iteration and feedback. If you

would like to discuss your writing at any time, stop by office hours or

reach out to the myself or a TA. Also, the  University Writing Center

(UWC) provides both  writing and speaking guides  for additional self-

study and has trained peer consultants available to support you on

your work.

Course Learning Outcomes

This course is intended to be an intensive programming experience that integrates

core concepts in Computer Science and familiarizes you with a variety of

programming/development tools and techniques. You will primarily work in small

(UWC) provides both  writing and speaking guides  for additional self-

study and has trained peer consultants available to support you on

your work.

Course Learning Outcomes

This course is intended to be an intensive programming experience that integrates

core concepts in Computer Science and familiarizes you with a variety of

programming/development tools and techniques. You will primarily work in small

teams on projects emphasizing different specializations within computer science.

The course focuses on honing good programming techniques to ease code

integration, reuse, and clarity. The primary goal for this class is for you to emerge

with strong programming skills and address individual and team programming

challenges competently. In this course, you will improve your programming skills

through significant practice.

After this course, you will be able to:

Explain  the need for software engineering through industry examples and

personal experience

Exercise  the fundamental concepts of software construction (including

managing requirements, design, implementation, testing, and deployment)

Design  and develop software that is clearer, more maintainable, and

integrates current software technologies – some that are new to you

Collaborate and communicate  effectively in small teams

Recognize and apply  characteristics of effective technical writing

We will cover many topics, including:

Software system design  for portability, performance, and testability

Coding  layout and style consideration

Programming  specifications and documentation

Use  of essential software tools and APIs

Subject-specific topics  related to the team projects (DB, HCI)

Though many topics will overlap, this course is not intended to be as in-depth or

comprehensive as a standard software engineering course. For a deeper

understanding of software development and project management, take software

engineering after completing this class.

Textbook and/or Resource Materials

This material Is: Required

No Textbook Needed

Notes:

Required textbooks and reading materials will be provided electronically through

Perusall and linked on Canvas.

To receive credit, each and all individual Perusall reading assignments must be

clicked on in Canvas. The grades will not transfer otherwise.

Grading Policy

Your grade for this course reflects your mastery of the course material and is

determined by multiple components. As a writing course,  you must pass the

writing components with a C or better to earn a grade in the course . Failure to

pass the writing components with a C or better results in an F for the course.

Specific grading practices will be announced when assignments are given through

assignment descriptions and assignment rubrics. When a rubric is given, the

determined by multiple components. As a writing course,  you must pass the

writing components with a C or better to earn a grade in the course . Failure to

pass the writing components with a C or better results in an F for the course.

Specific grading practices will be announced when assignments are given through

assignment descriptions and assignment rubrics. When a rubric is given, the

assignment must meet  Minimum Viable Solution  requirements before being

assessed by the rubric. The rubric is not the assignment, and rubrics can be

adjusted to more closely reflect the assignment or items being assessed. When in

doubt, follow the assignment.

Individual Project  – 5%: A project about HTML, CSS, and JavaScript.

Team Project 1 – 16%: A project about Databases. Teams must use the

Waterfall Software Development Model

30% from incremental assessments.

70% from the instructor's assessment of the delivered product.

Team Project 2 – 36%: A continuation of Team Project 1, which adds Cloud

and Web Programming. Teams must use the Agile/Scrum Software

Development Model.

30% from incremental assessments

70% from the instructor's assessment of the delivered product.

Written Elements  – 33%: This is a writing-intensive course. You must pass

this component with a C or better to earn a grade in the course. You will have

several writing assignments for the projects.

Foundational Course Activities – 10% : The course has several different

foundational types of activities to help you better understand concepts and

connect with the material. This includes (but is not limited to) reading, in-class

activities, and lab activities. These activities bring deeper, longer-term learning

as you work, either individually or with other students. You must be present

for in-class and lab activities to receive credit. For submitted assignments,

grading will be based on the quality of the submitted work. (Note: EACH and

All individual Perusall reading assignments must be clicked on in Canvas to

receive credit. The grades will not transfer otherwise.)

This component will be divided as follows:

25% in-class activities

25% lab activities

15% at-home activities

10% in-class recall practice

25% Team Success Contributions

Team Success Contributions

Your individual contribution to the success of the team projects and group

assignments will be graded. In any team environment, striving to be a valuable team

member is critical. Logged code commits, peer evaluation, attendance,

participation, and instructor/TA observations will be evaluated for this grade. Failure

to honestly and accurately participate in the peer evaluation process (giving

evaluations and/or reflecting on evaluations) can negatively impact your individual

contribution score. An example of not participating honestly and accurately in peer

member is critical. Logged code commits, peer evaluation, attendance,

participation, and instructor/TA observations will be evaluated for this grade. Failure

to honestly and accurately participate in the peer evaluation process (giving

evaluations and/or reflecting on evaluations) can negatively impact your individual

contribution score. An example of not participating honestly and accurately in peer

evaluations is to give all top scores (like all 5's on a 0-5 scale) when the project is

behind, lacking in quality, or a member promises to catch up for a better evaluation.

Minimum Requirement for Credit on Assignments

In order to receive credit for an assignment, a good faith effort must be applied,

resulting in a submission that matches the requirements of the assignment and

earns a score of at least 50%. Submissions that fail to meet this standard will

receive a grade of 0. You must be present for in-class activities or recall practices.

For group assignments, your full in-person participation is required to receive

a grade for that assignment.

Letter Grade Reduction for Failing a Grading Component

Students who earn less than 70% credit for any grading component (projects,

written elements, or individual foundational course activities) will receive a letter

grade reduction.

Lab sessions are an essential time for working with your team. Missing 6 or more lab

sessions (which constitutes 20% of scheduled labs) will result in a reduction of your

final course letter grade.

Grading Scale

Grades will be assigned according to the following scale:

A  ≥  90 > B  ≥  80 > C  ≥  70 > D  ≥  60 > F

These grades represent varying degrees of achievement (see the university's

grading system):

A = excellent, B = good, C = satisfactory, D = passing, F = failing

Late Work Policy

Work submitted by a student as makeup work for an excused absence is not

considered late work and is exempt from the late work policy ( Student Rule 7 ).

Course Specific Late Work Policy

Assignments turned in after the posted deadline will have a penalty of 5% per day

late.

After 10 days, late work is not accepted.

For team assignments, the late penalty affects the grade for the entire team.

If the assignment is individual, the late penalty only affects the grade for that

team member.

Complete any late or makeup work before the Thanksgiving break. After this,

no late or make-up work will be accepted.

Note On Finals

There are 2 finals associated with this class, one for your lecture time and one for

your lab time. You are required to come to both final times with your team.

Additional Course Information

Copyright

The materials used within this course are copyrighted. These materials include, but

are not limited to, the syllabus, quizzes, assignments, activity descriptions, online

content, course videos, audio and visual recordings of classes, etc. Because these

materials are copyrighted, you do not have the right to copy or distribute these

materials, unless permission is expressly granted.

Communication

We will use Canvas to post assignments, course resources, and discussions and

send announcements. You are responsible for checking Canvas and paying attention

to class emails and announcements. Let myself or a TA know immediately if you do

not have access to Canvas or are not receiving announcements so we can resolve

the issue.

When emailing, it is best to include both myself and your TA. If you don't hear back

within two business days, please send a follow up email. We will appreciate the kind

reminder.

BYOD

Bring Your Own Device  (BYOD) is an initiative in the College of Engineering where

not have access to Canvas or are not receiving announcements so we can resolve

the issue.

When emailing, it is best to include both myself and your TA. If you don't hear back

within two business days, please send a follow up email. We will appreciate the kind

reminder.

BYOD

Bring Your Own Device  (BYOD) is an initiative in the College of Engineering where

students are required to bring their own computing devices to lectures and labs. For

this cousre, you need a computing device with a headset, microphone, and webcam.

You will also need stable internet access outside of lectures and labs. BYOD devices

fulfill the requirements for this course. The BYOD webpage answers many common

questions including those on financial assistance.

Submission of Assignments

All assignments will be turned in electronically through Canvas (unless otherwise

specified) by the due date and time given on the assignment. Email submissions will

not be accepted.

Course Specific Attendance Policy

Course - Specific   Attendance   and   Participation   Policy

Success   in   this   course   depends   not   just   on   completing   assignments ,

but   on   active   participation   in   class   and   lab   activities .  Many   important

learning   opportunities — including   group   work ,  discussions ,  hands - on

labs ,  and   AI   demonstrations — take   place   during   scheduled   sessions .

Your   regular   presence   and   engagement   are   essential   to   mastering   the

material   and   to   your   success   as   a   future   software   engineer .

In - class / lab   activities   can   only   be   completed   by   students   who   are

present .  No   credit   will   be   given   for   in - class   or   lab   activities   unless

you   attend   and   participate .

Missing   6  or   more   lab   sessions  ( which   constitutes  20%  of

scheduled   labs )  will   result   in   a   reduction   of   your   final   course   letter

grade .

We   recognize   that   occasional   absences   are   sometimes   unavoidable .  If

you   must   miss   a   class   or   lab   for   a   legitimate ,  documented   reason ,

please   review   Student   Rule  7   and   communicate   with   the   instructor   as

soon   as   possible .

By   attending   class   and   labs   consistently ,  you   are   investing   in   your   own

learning   and   success .  I   look   forward   to   your   enthusiastic   participation !

AI Statement

AI   Policy   Statement   for   Software   Engineering

Artificial   Intelligence  ( AI )  tools — such   as   ChatGPT ,  Copilot ,  and   others —

are   rapidly   changing   the   practice   of   software   engineering .  In   this

course ,  you   will   be   both   required   and   encouraged   to   use   AI   in

AI Statement

AI   Policy   Statement   for   Software   Engineering

Artificial   Intelligence  ( AI )  tools — such   as   ChatGPT ,  Copilot ,  and   others —

are   rapidly   changing   the   practice   of   software   engineering .  In   this

course ,  you   will   be   both   required   and   encouraged   to   use   AI   in

structured ,  intentional   ways   to   deepen   your   understanding   of   how

these   tools   affect   your   workflow   and   outputs .

Permitted   and   Required   Uses   of   AI

Certain   assignments   will   direct   you   to   use   AI   tools   for   specific   tasks

( e . g .,  generating   code   comments ,  comparing   your   code   to   AI -

generated   code ,  or   improving   writing ).  Follow   all   instructions   for

these   assignments   precisely ,  including   any   prompt   engineering   or

documentation   requirements .

When   AI   tools   are   required   for   an   assignment ,  you   must   cite   the

tool   used ,  include   your   prompts   and   the   AI ’ s   responses ,  and   reflect

on   the   results   as   instructed .

General   Guidance

AI   may   be   used   to   assist   with   limited ,  well - defined   tasks  ( such   as

debugging   or   suggesting   code   comments ),  but   may   not   be   used   to

design   your   entire   project   or   to   complete   assignments   whose

primary   learning   outcomes   involve   your   own   design   and

implementation .

Direct   use   of   AI   to   generate   complete   solutions   for   programming

assignments ,  project   designs ,  or   other   major   tasks   is   prohibited

unless   explicitly   stated   otherwise   in   the   assignment   instructions .

Academic   Integrity

If   you   do   use   AI   in   any   part   of   your   work ,  including   explicit

assignment   directions ,  you   must   clearly   cite   the   tool ,  document

prompts ,  and   output ,  and   explain   how   you   used   it .  Failure   to   do   so

will   be   considered   academic   misconduct .

Be   aware :  misusing   AI   can   limit   your   learning   and   preparation   for

assessments .  Historically ,  students   who   over - relied   on   AI

performed   poorly   on   independent   assessments .

If   you   are   uncertain   whether   a   particular   use   of   AI   is   allowed ,  ask   the

instructor   before   proceeding .

This   policy   is   intended   to   help   you   learn   how   to   use   modern   AI   tools

responsibly   and   ethically   in   software   engineering .
